title: AI-Powered Lawyering: AI Reasoning Models, Retrieval Augmented Generation, and the Future of Legal Practice
venue: Journal of Law and Empirical Analysis
study_design_description: This is a randomized controlled trial assessing AI technologies for legal work. Upper-level law students from the University of Minnesota and University of Michigan were randomly assigned to one of three groups of equal size. Participants completed six realistic legal tasks remotely from October 1-31, 2024, using a Canvas interface. Three co-authors evaluated all work product using a blind grading process to ensure graders were unaware of experimental condition and participant identity. The study was preregistered with the American Economic Association's registry for randomized controlled trials.
primary_task_description: Participants completed six realistic legal tasks developed in collaboration with practicing lawyers, in order: (1) Draft an email for a client explaining legal precedents (60-min limit); (2) Draft a legal memo on a complex contract-interpretation issue (240-min limit); (3) Analyze a complaint and draft written analysis (120-min limit); (4) Draft a nondisclosure agreement (NDA) by adapting a template (180-min limit); (5) Draft a motion to consolidate meeting court's formal requirements (150-min limit); (6) Draft a persuasive letter evaluating enforceability of a covenant not to compete (150-min limit). Participants were instructed to complete tasks in order and report time spent on each task.
population_description: The study evaluates 137 law students from the University of Minnesota and the University of Michigan. Recruitment emails were sent to all second- and third-year law students, as well as Master of Laws (LL.M.) students. More than 250 students expressed interest, 153 formally enrolled, 125 completed all tasks, and 137 completed at least one task. The sample included 60 2L students, 60 3L students, and 17 LL.M. students. Mean GPA was approximately 3.3 across groups (120 participants with GPA data). Demographic and academic information was collected during enrollment, including class year, first-year law school GPA, and prior use of generative AI tools in the three months prior to enrollment.

arms.description: No AI assistance; participants prohibited from using any generative AI
Access to o1-preview, a general-purpose AI reasoning model released by OpenAI in September 2024

Access to Vincent AI (VLex), a specialized AI tool for lawyers that uses RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation) to facilitate legal work. At the time of the study, Vincent AI used an ensemble of non-reasoning models, including GPT-4 and 4o, as its underlying foundation models

outcomes.outcome_description: Average of five core quality attributes (Accuracy, Analysis, Organization, Clarity, Professionalism) measured on a standard 1-7 scale
The precision and usefulness of the research, measured on a standard 1-7 scale
The depth and insightfulness of the reasoning, measured on a standard 1-7 scale
The structure of the work product, measured on a standard 1-7 scale
The quality and persuasiveness of the writing, measured on a standard 1-7 scale
The extent to which directions were followed, measured on a standard 1-7 scale
Binary metric for whether any cited sources or assertions appear to be hallucinated, either because they do not exist or because their descriptions are entirely inaccurate
The fraction of the task's allotted time spent completing the task, so that lower values correspond to faster task completion
Task-level productivity defined as the sum of the five quality attribute scores divided by the fraction of allotted time spent

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: vincent_ai vs control
reported_statistics.regression_coefficient: β = 0.26*** (SE 0.10) without participant FEs; β = 0.27*** (SE 0.10) with participant FEs
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: Vincent AI improves quality by 0.26*** (0.10) points without participant FEs and 0.27*** (0.10) with participant FEs.

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: o1_preview vs control
reported_statistics.regression_coefficient: β = 0.52*** (SE 0.09) without participant FEs; β = 0.53*** (SE 0.09) with participant FEs
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: o1-preview improves quality by 0.52*** (0.09) points without participant FEs and 0.53*** (0.09) with participant FEs.

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: o1_preview vs vincent_ai
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: A direct test of equality confirms that the larger o1-preview estimated effect is statistically distinguishable from the Vincent AI estimated effect at conventional levels: the 0.26-point difference between the two coefficients yields a t-statistic of approximately 1.9.

notes: Participants were upper-level law students, not fully licensed lawyers, which raises questions about generalizability to practicing attorneys. The study was conducted at two selective law schools (Minnesota and Michigan), so results may not extend to students at less selective institutions. Tasks were predominantly litigation-oriented; only the NDA task was transactional. Vincent AI was designed primarily for litigation tasks. The study could not assess long-term effects of AI use or learning curves. Task order was not counterbalanced, though authors doubt this significantly biased results. Grading was blind but inherently subjective despite pre-specified rubrics. Demand effects are possible since participants knew when they had AI access. The paper notes that neither tool improved accuracy overall, and neither tool improved performance on the NDA or legal memo tasks (though Vincent AI showed a positive but non-significant point estimate on the memo). Authors suggest AI tools primarily 'raise the floor' by narrowing performance gaps rather than uniformly shifting the distribution upward. Baseline skill analyses are susceptible to regression-to-the-mean effects and should be interpreted with caution. The study was preregistered with the AEA RCT registry. Randomization balance checks showed no significant differences across groups in GPA (F-test p = 0.731), student type (Chi-sq p = 0.832), AI use (Chi-sq p = 0.903), or school (Chi-sq p = 0.793).
